paper_title: Respiratory Symptoms and Pollen Exposure Study

methods.description: Analysis of the association between pollen exposure and respiratory symptom scores using Generalized Additive Mixed Models (GAMMs) with quasi-Poisson distribution, accounting for temporal correlation and adjusting for covariates. This method allows for assessing both linear and nonlinear relationships.

steps.description: The association of pollen exposure with daytime and nighttime respiratory symptom scores was assessed using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) with quasi-Poisson distribution. GAMMs provide a general framework for extending linear mixed models. They allow the assessment of nonlinear relationships between exposure and outcome by using smooth functions.
steps.searchable_summary: Define statistical model framework (GAMM, quasi-Poisson) for association analysis

steps.description: Random effect and autoregressive correlation structure (lag 1) were employed to account for temporal correlation of respiratory symptoms within each subject.
steps.searchable_summary: Specify random effects and temporal correlation structure (AR(1)) for GAMM
inputs: Time series structure of symptom scores

steps.description: Separate models were built for combined, tree, and grass pollen.
steps.searchable_summary: Fit separate GAMM models for combined, tree, and grass pollen exposure
outputs: Fitted GAMM models (initial) for each pollen type

steps.description: All models were adjusted for a priori selected risk factors identified in previous studies of this cohort: PM 2.5 , NO 2 , siblings, childcare, sex, breastfeeding, age, maternal smoking during pregnancy, temperature, and month (to control for seasonal variation of respiratory symptoms). In addition, models (except combined pollen models) were mutually adjusted for tree pollen and grass pollen.
steps.searchable_summary: Adjust GAMM models with a priori covariates and mutual pollen adjustment
inputs: Covariate data (PM2.5, NO2, siblings, childcare, sex, breastfeeding, age, maternal smoking, temperature, month)

steps.description: Pollen groups and covariates were included as smoothing terms in case of a nonlinear relationship with respiratory symptom scores. If a nonlinear relationship has been identified, the sample was restricted to the range of pollen concentrations linearly associated with the outcome in order to obtain interpretable coefficient estimates.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and handle nonlinear relationships using smoothing terms and sample restriction

steps.description: To evaluate effect modification by maternal atopy or infant's sex, separate models including factor-smooth interaction terms were fitted. In order to assess effect modification by NO 2 and PM 2.5 , separate models were fitted including tensor product interaction terms (ti).
steps.searchable_summary: Fit GAMM models with interaction terms to assess effect modification by maternal atopy, infant sex, NO2, and PM2.5
inputs: Potential effect modifiers (maternal atopy, infant sex, NO2, PM2.5)
outputs: Fitted GAMM models with interaction terms

steps.description: In addition, three sensitivity analyses were performed. First, the association between pollen and respiratory symptoms was investigated considering additional risk factors: cat, dog, delivery mode, birthweight, maternal education, maternal atopy, season of birth, study center (to control for intercenter differences), and relative humidity.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform sensitivity analysis by including additional covariates in separate models
inputs: Additional covariates (cat, dog, delivery mode, birthweight, maternal education, maternal atopy, season of birth, study center, relative humidity)

steps.description: Estimates are expressed as risk ratios (RRs) per 10% increase in pollen/m 3 (RR = 1.10 β ). An RR of 1.006 can be interpreted as a 0.6% increase in respiratory symptom score for an increase in pollen concentration by 10%.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate and express model estimates as Risk Ratios (RRs) per 10% pollen increase

methods.description: Process for selecting the study population from the Basel-Bern Infant Lung Development (BILD) cohort based on specific inclusion and exclusion criteria, data availability, and ethical considerations.

steps.description: Data were collected from the Basel-Bern Infant Lung Development (BILD) cohort, an ongoing prospective birth cohort of unselected healthy infants.
steps.searchable_summary: Access data from the BILD cohort

steps.description: Exclusion criteria for the cohort study are ethnicity other than Caucasian, major birth defects, perinatal diseases, and major respiratory disease diagnosed after birth.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter cohort data based on general BILD cohort exclusion criteria
inputs: General BILD cohort exclusion criteria (ethnicity, birth defects, perinatal diseases, respiratory disease)

steps.description: Because air pollution data were only available from January 1, 2005, to January 1, 2017, solely infants enrolled in the BILD cohort between January 1, 2005, and January 1, 2016, were included in the present study.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter participants based on enrollment date range (Jan 1, 2005 to Jan 1, 2016)

steps.description: For the present study, the following additional exclusion criteria applied: preterm birth (< 37 weeks), <50 symptom scores assessed during the 1 st year of life (to ensure a comparable number of observations among infants), moved abroad, and missing data ( Figure S1).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply study-specific exclusion criteria (preterm birth, minimum symptom score count, location status, missing data)
inputs: Study-specific exclusion criteria (gestational age < 37 weeks, symptom score count < 50, moved abroad status, data completeness requirements)

steps.description: The Ethics Committee of Northwest and Central Switzerland (EKNZ, Basel, Switzerland) and the Bernese Cantonal Ethics Research Committee (KEK, Bern, Switzerland) approved the study protocol, and the written informed consent was obtained from the parents at enrollment.
steps.searchable_summary: Document ethical approval and informed consent process

steps.description: We included in total 401 term-born infants with complete data for 14,874 measurement time points to analyze the association between pollen and respiratory symptoms during the first year of life ( Figure S1).
steps.searchable_summary: Count final number of participants and measurement time points included in the study
outputs: Final sample size (N infants, N time points)

steps.description: Respiratory symptom scores were overall low ( Table 1 and Table S2). The mean ± SD symptom score from January through September across all years was 0.13 ± 0.13 for daytime respiratory symptoms and 0.12 ± 0.14 for nighttime respiratory symptoms. Within these months, infants on average ± SD had daytime respiratory symptoms for 3.92 ± 3.77 weeks and nighttime respira- Table 2 and Table S1.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate and report descriptive statistics for respiratory symptom scores in the study population
inputs: Respiratory symptom scores from final study population dataset
outputs: Summary statistics (mean, SD) for symptom scores
